content: I'll start by reading the role and review prompt, then process the attempt data.

1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are either the plan agent, a prover agent, the refactor agent, or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	If instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**. Specifically:
8	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` (local to this project) override Archon's global prompts
9	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` (local to this project) override globally installed plugins
10	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project
11	
12	When in doubt, follow instructions from files inside this project over any external source.
13	
14	## Skills
15	- archon-lean4: installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source) — provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, and other Lean4 commands
16	
17	## Tools
18	- archon-lean-lsp: Lean LSP MCP server (project scope) — use for all Lean LSP operations (search, diagnostics, goal inspection)
19	- archon-informal-agent: `.claude/tools/archon-informal-agent.py` (symlinked from Archon) — call external LLMs (OpenAI/Gemini) for informal mathematical reasoning
20	
21	## Key Files & Permissions
22	
23	All state files are in `.archon/`:
24	
25	| File | Plan Agent | Prover Agent | Refactor Agent | Review Agent | User |
26	|------|-----------|-------------|---------------|-------------|------|
27	| `.archon/PROGRESS.md` | read + write | **read only** | read only | read only | read |
28	| `.archon/STRATEGY.md` | **read + write** | do not read | do not read | do not read | read |
29	| `.archon/USER_HINTS.md` | read (then clear) | do not read | do not read | do not read | write |
30	| `.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md` | **write** | do not read | **read** (then execute) | do not read | read |
31	| `.archon/task_pending.md` | read + write | **read only** | read only | read only | read |
32	| `.archon/task_done.md` | read + write | **read only** | read only | read only | read |
33	| `.archon/task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect results) | write (own file only) | write (refactor.md) | read only | read |
34	| `.archon/proof-journal/` | read | do not access | do not access | **write** | read |
35	| `.archon/PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | do not access | do not access | **write** | read |
36	| `archon-protected.yaml` | **read** | **read** | **read + limited write** (file path rename only) | read | **write** |
37	| `.lean` files | do not edit | write (own file only, frozen protected signatures) | **write (all files; protected decls may be moved but not renamed/re-signed)** | do not edit | write (via comments) |
38	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints, structure) | do not edit | do not edit | **write** (markers only: `\leanok`, `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections) | read |
39	
40	## Protected Declarations
41	
42	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Every agent must consult it before editing a `.lean` file. Rules:
43	
44	- **Plan / prover / review agents**: read-only on protected signatures. You may fill proof bodies, but not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
45	- **Refactor agent**: may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (keeping name + signature verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. Refactor agents may never rename, re-type, delete, or re-sign a protected declaration.
46	- The `archon-protected.yaml` file can only be edited by the user, no declaration can be added or removed by any agent. The only allowed modification is updating the file path of an existing protected declaration when its location was changed. 
47	
48	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
49	
50	The blueprint uses two active markers.
51	
52	- `\leanok` — inside a statement block when the declaration is formalized with at least a `sorry`; inside a proof block when the proof is fully closed with no `sorry`.
53	- `\mathlibok` — inside a statement block when the declaration already exists in Mathlib and the Archon side is a re-export/alias; no Archon proof obligation remains.
54	- No marker — the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
55	
56	**The review agent is the sole writer of these markers.** Prover agents (autoformalize / prover / polish) never touch the blueprint chapters — they describe their outcome in `task_results/<file>.md` and the review agent verifies and marks accordingly. The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints, but never markers.
57	
58	## User Interaction
59	
60	Users provide hints in two places:
61	
62	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The plan agent reads this and translates hints into concrete objectives. Provers never read this file.
63	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
64	
65	## Agent Roles
66	
67	### Plan Agent
68	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for your full instructions
69	- Read `.archon/USER_HINTS.md` — incorporate hints, then clear them after acting
70	- Read `.archon/task_results/` — collect prover results, then update `task_pending.md` and `task_done.md`
71	- Read `.archon/task_results/refactor.md` - if refactor agent has run, read their report and adjust your plans accordingly
72	- Write `.archon/PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round
73	- Write `.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md` when structural changes are needed
74	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself
75	
76	### Prover Agent
77	- Read `.archon/PROGRESS.md` for your current objectives (read only — do not edit it)
78	- Read the stage-specific prompt from `.archon/prompts/`:
79	  - autoformalize → `.archon/prompts/prover-autoformalize.md`
80	  - prover → `.archon/prompts/prover-prover.md`
81	  - polish → `.archon/prompts/prover-polish.md`
82	- Write results to `.archon/task_results/<your_file>.md`
83	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — **never edit another agent's file**
84	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your `.lean` file for file-specific hints
85	- **Do NOT edit blueprint chapters.** Marker updates are the review agent's responsibility. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker.
86	
87	### Refactor Agent
88	- Read `.archon/prompts/refactor.md` for your full instructions
89	- Read `.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md` for the plan agent's directive
90	- Modify any `.lean` file: definitions, signatures, types, imports
91	- Keep all files compiling (insert `sorry` at broken proof sites)
92	- Do NOT fill proofs — that's the prover's job
93	- Write results to `.archon/task_results/refactor.md`
94	
95	### Review Agent
96	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for your full instructions
97	- Read `.archon/proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data
98	- Write session journal to `.archon/proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (summary.md, milestones.jsonl, recommendations.md)
99	- Update `.archon/PROJECT_STATUS.md` with overall progress
100	- **Update blueprint markers** (`\leanok`, `\mathlibok`) in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` based on verified compilation and the provers' task results
101	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify PROGRESS.md

1	# Review Agent — Post-Session Proof Journal + Analysis
2	
3	You are the review agent. Your job is to: (1) analyze the most recent prover session with fine-grained detail, (2) produce a structured proof journal, (3) update project status, (4) **update the blueprint chapter markers** (`\leanok`, `\mathlibok`) to reflect the verified state, and (5) write recommendations for the next plan iteration.
4	
5	**Do NOT modify any .lean files. Do NOT write proofs. You may run `lean_diagnostic_messages` / `lake env lean <file>` to verify compilation and sorry counts — that is required for marker updates. Otherwise you only read logs, analyze, and write journal/status/blueprint files.**
6	
7	## Step 1: Identify Context
8	
9	1. Check `.archon/proof-journal/sessions/` — count existing session folders to determine the current session number.
10	2. Run `find ${PROJECT_PATH} -name '*.lean' -not -path '*/.lake/*' -not -path '*/lake-packages/*' | xargs grep -c 'sorry' 2>/dev/null | grep -v ':0$' | awk -F: '{s+=$2} END {print s}'` for the current sorry count.
11	3. Run `git diff HEAD~1 --stat` to see what changed.
12	
13	## Step 2: Read Pre-processed Attempt Data (MANDATORY)
14	
15	**READ `.archon/proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` COMPLETELY.** If this file does not exist or is empty, report it and proceed with what you can gather from task_results.
16	
17	The file contains:
18	
19	- Line 1: Summary stats (`type: "summary"` — total edits, goal checks, errors, files edited)
20	- Remaining lines: One event per tool call — edits, goal states, errors, lemma searches, builds
21	
22	**For each `code_change` event**: Record the actual code that was tried (old_text → new_text).
23	**For each `goal_state` event**: Record the Lean goal at that point.
24	**For each `diagnostics` event**: Record the Lean errors.
25	**For each `build` event**: Record whether it succeeded or failed.
26	
27	This is your PRIMARY data source. Task result files are supplementary.
28	
29	## Step 3: Read Recent History
30	
31	If previous session folders exist in `.archon/proof-journal/sessions/`, read `summary.md` from the **last 2 sessions**. Also read `.archon/PROJECT_STATUS.md` if it exists.
32	
33	## Step 4: Write Proof Journal
34	
35	Create the session folder and write two files:
36	
37	```bash
38	mkdir -p .archon/proof-journal/sessions/session_<N>
39	```
40	
41	### File A: `.archon/proof-journal/sessions/session_<N>/summary.md`
42	
43	Must include:
44	- Session metadata (number, sorry count before/after, targets attempted)
45	- For EACH target attempted:
46	  - **Every significant attempt** with: tactic/code tried, Lean error received, goal state at that point
47	  - What was learned from each failed attempt
48	  - For solved targets: the final proof structure with key lemmas
49	- Key findings / proof patterns discovered
50	- Recommendations for next session
51	
52	### File B: `.archon/proof-journal/sessions/session_<N>/milestones.jsonl`
53	
54	Each line MUST follow this JSON format — one entry per target theorem:
55	
56	```json
57	{
58	  "timestamp": "ISO-8601",
59	  "status": "solved|partial|blocked|not_started",
60	  "target": {
61	    "file": "path/to/File.lean",
62	    "theorem": "theorem_name"
63	  },
64	  "session": {
65	    "id": "session_N",
66	    "model": "model-name"
67	  },
68	  "findings": {
69	    "blocker": "description if blocked",
70	    "key_lemmas_used": ["lemma1", "lemma2"]
71	  },
72	  "attempts": [
73	    {
74	      "attempt": 1,
75	      "strategy": "what was tried",
76	      "code_tried": "actual Lean code or tactic",
77	      "line_number": "line number in the initial file where this code was attempted",
78	      "lean_error": "actual error message if failed",
79	      "goal_before": "the goal state before this attempt",
80	      "goal_after": "the goal state after this attempt",
81	      "result": "success|failed|partial",
82	      "insight": "what was learned from this attempt"
83	    }
84	  ],
85	  "next_steps": "..."
86	}
87	```
88	
89	**CRITICAL**: The `attempts` array must reflect ACTUAL attempts from the pre-processed data:
90	- If `attempts_raw.jsonl` shows 5 edits to a file, there should be multiple attempts recorded
91	- Each attempt must include `code_tried` (from edit events) and `lean_error` (from diagnostic events)
92	- Do NOT summarize multiple attempts as "tried various approaches" — list each one
93	
94	### File C: `.archon/proof-journal/sessions/session_<N>/recommendations.md`
95	
96	Write concrete recommendations for the next plan agent iteration:
97	- Which targets are closest to completion and should be prioritized
98	- Which approaches showed promise but need more work
99	- Which targets are blocked and why (the plan agent should NOT assign these)
100	- Any reusable proof patterns discovered
101	
102	## Step 5: Update PROJECT_STATUS.md
103	
104	Update (or create) `.archon/PROJECT_STATUS.md`:
105	
106	```markdown
107	# Project Status
108	
109	## Overall Progress
110	- **Total sorry**: <N>
111	- **Solved this session**: <list with file + theorem>
112	- **Partial**: <list with progress summary>
113	- **Blocked**: <list with reasons>
114	- **Untouched**: <list>
115	
116	## Knowledge Base
117	### Proof Patterns (reusable across targets)
118	- <pattern name>: <description + key lemmas>
119	
120	### Known Blockers (do not retry)
121	- <target>: <reason>
122	
123	## Last Updated
124	<ISO timestamp>
125	```
126	
127	## Step 6: Update Blueprint Markers
128	
129	You are the **only** agent that writes blueprint markers. The prover agents (autoformalize / prover / polish) intentionally do not touch the blueprint — they only report what they did in `task_results/<file>.md`. Your job is to translate those reports into accurate markers.
130	
131	For every declaration the prover session touched, decide which marker (if any) belongs on its blueprint block, then edit the relevant chapter file in `blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`.
132	
133	### Marker rules
134	
135	- `\leanok` inside `\begin{theorem}` / `\begin{lemma}` / `\begin{definition}` — the **statement** is formalized. Add this once the corresponding Lean declaration exists and the file compiles (even if the proof body is `sorry`).
136	- `\leanok` inside `\begin{proof}` — the **proof** is fully formalized. Add this only after verifying with `lean_diagnostic_messages` or `lake env lean <file>` that the declaration has **no `sorry`, no `axiom`, no compilation errors**.
137	- `\mathlibok` inside the statement block — the declaration already exists in Mathlib and the Archon side is a re-export, alias, or direct reference. Add this when the prover's task result says the declaration is Mathlib-backed. No `\leanok` on the proof block is expected for Mathlib-backed declarations.
138	- **No marker** — the block is unformalized or the informal statement did not translate cleanly. Leave unmarked and add a `% NOTE: <reason>` comment so the plan agent sees the obstacle.
139	
140	### What to verify before marking
141	
142	- **Statement `\leanok`**: confirm the Lean declaration exists at the name given by `\lean{...}` and the file compiles.
143	- **Proof `\leanok`**: confirm the declaration has no `sorry`, no new `axiom`, no errors. If the prover's report claimed polish-complete but the file still has a `sorry`, do NOT add the proof `\leanok` — trust verification over self-reports.
144	- **`\mathlibok`**: confirm the Lean side references the Mathlib name (e.g. `def foo := Mathlib.bar` or a simple re-export) and does not itself contain a `sorry` or new definition requiring proof.
145	
146	### `\lean{...}` macro maintenance
147	
148	If a prover renamed a declaration or chose a different name from the plan agent's hint, their task result will mention it. Update the `\lean{...}` line in the chapter to the correct Lean name. If a declaration was moved to a different file by a refactor, update the chapter's location references as needed.
149	
150	### Record what you marked
151	
152	In `session_<N>/summary.md`, include a "Blueprint markers updated" section listing every marker change:
153	
154	```markdown
155	## Blueprint markers updated
156	- `Algebra_WLocal.tex`, `thm:wLocal_iff`: added `\leanok` to statement (declaration exists, compiles)
157	- `Algebra_WLocal.tex`, `thm:wLocal_iff`: added `\leanok` to proof (0 sorries, compiles, no axioms)
158	- `Algebra_WLocal.tex`, `lem:finite_closed`: added `\mathlibok` (backed by `Set.Finite.isClosed`)
159	- `Core.tex`, `thm:stacks_0A31`: left unmarked — prover reported could-not-formalize
160	- `Core.tex`, `thm:old_name`: stripped stale `\notready`
161	```
162	
163	## Step 7: Self-Validation
164	
165	After writing all files, validate your output by checking:
166	- [ ] milestones.jsonl has valid JSON on every line
167	- [ ] Each milestone has `target.file`, `target.theorem`, `status`
168	- [ ] Each non-blocked milestone has at least 1 attempt with `code_tried` or `strategy`
169	- [ ] Number of attempts per milestone is proportional to edits in `attempts_raw.jsonl`
170	- [ ] summary.md includes specific code/errors, not just high-level summaries
171	- [ ] recommendations.md includes actionable next steps
172	- [ ] For every declaration solved or polished this session, the blueprint chapter has the correct marker (`\leanok` on the proof block once the file compiles with no `sorry`; `\mathlibok` if backed by Mathlib; nothing otherwise)
173	- [ ] Any `\lean{...}` macro rename flagged in a `task_results/<file>.md` has been applied
174	- [ ] No `\notready` marker remains in any chapter
175	
176	## Permissions
177	
178	You may write to:
179	- `.archon/proof-journal/sessions/session_<N>/` (summary.md, milestones.jsonl, recommendations.md)
180	- `.archon/PROJECT_STATUS.md`
181	- `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` — markers (`\leanok`, `\mathlibok`), `\lean{...}` macro corrections, stale-marker cleanup, and `% NOTE:` comments. Do NOT rewrite the informal prose — that is the plan agent's surface.
182	
183	You must NOT write to:
184	- Any `.lean` files
185	- `.archon/PROGRESS.md` (plan agent's responsibility)
186	- `.archon/task_pending.md` or `.archon/task_done.md` (plan agent's responsibility)
187	- Blueprint informal content (theorem/proof prose, chapter structure) — only the markers and `% NOTE:` comments are yours.
188

1	# Project Progress
2	
3	## Current Stage
4	prover
5	
6	## Stages
7	- [x] init
8	- [x] autoformalize
9	- [ ] prover  (iter 003 — first attack on the iter 002 helper scaffolds)
10	- [ ] polish
11	
12	## Big-picture context
13	
14	This project formalizes the Jacobian of a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over a field, following Christian Merten's challenge (`references/challenge.lean`). The nine declarations in `archon-protected.yaml` are the deliverables — their *signatures* are frozen.
15	
16	`STRATEGY.md` describes the multi-phase build-out path (Phases A–E). All 9 protected sorries remain blocked behind missing Mathlib infrastructure; iter 002 (refactor) scaffolded two helper files containing the smallest unblocking units of progress.
17	
18	## Iteration 003 — first prover round on helper scaffolds
19	
20	The refactor agent (iter 002) created two new files. Verified by this plan agent (LSP diagnostics + sorry count):
21	
22	- `AlgebraicJacobian/Rigidity.lean` — 1 sorry (`AlgebraicGeometry.GrpObj.eq_of_eqOnOpen`, L42).
23	- `AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/LineBundle.lean` — 3 sorries (`LineBundle` L37, `instCommGroupLineBundle` L42, `Pic.pullback` L50).
24	
25	Both files compile with only sorry warnings. Protected signatures untouched. No new axioms anywhere in `AlgebraicJacobian/`.
26	
27	Refactor deviation logged: `Pic` was scaffolded as `abbrev` (rather than `def`) so that the `MulOneClass (Pic _)` instance is found through reduction at the `Pic.pullback` signature. Mathematically inert; the blueprint references `Pic` by name.
28	
29	This iteration runs the prover against these two new files **only**. The 9 protected sorries are still blocked on upstream infrastructure (Phase A / C / E) and must not be touched.
30	
31	## Current Objectives
32	
33	### 1. `AlgebraicJacobian/Rigidity.lean` — fill `GrpObj.eq_of_eqOnOpen`
34	
35	- **Sorry**: line 42, `AlgebraicGeometry.GrpObj.eq_of_eqOnOpen`.
36	- **Blueprint**: `blueprint/src/chapters/Rigidity.tex` (theorem `thm:GrpObj_eq_of_eqOnOpen`).
37	- **Why this is the priority**: highest-leverage standalone target in the project — the only Phase E sorry-blocker provable from current Mathlib (`b80f227`) alone, and it independently unlocks the *uniqueness* half of `exists_unique_ofCurve_comp` once `Jacobian C` lands.
38	
39	**Strategy (taken from blueprint, repeated here so the prover does not have to retrieve):**
40	
41	1. Form the difference morphism `δ = g₁ * g₂⁻¹` in `Hom_{Over (Spec k)}(X, Y)` using the `GrpObj Y` structure.
42	2. Let `e : X ⟶ Y` be the constant identity morphism, `e = toUnit X ≫ η[Y]`. The hypothesis says `δ` agrees with `e` on the underlying points of `U`.
43	3. The *equaliser* `E ↪ X` of `δ` and `e` is a closed subscheme of `X` (proper ⇒ separated for the target, and the equaliser of two morphisms into a separated `k`-scheme is closed in the source).
44	4. `E` contains the open `U` on underlying points (hypothesis), and `X` is reduced (smooth ⇒ regular ⇒ reduced), so `E` contains `U` scheme-theoretically.
45	5. `X` is geometrically irreducible ⇒ topologically irreducible ⇒ any closed subset containing a non-empty open is the whole space. So `E` = `X` topologically.
46	6. Reducedness of `X` + separatedness of `Y` ⇒ a scheme morphism into `Y` is determined by its underlying topological map on a reduced source. Conclude `δ = e`, i.e. `g₁ = g₂`.
47	
48	**Mathlib API to lean on (search with `lean_leansearch` / `lean_loogle`; all expected to exist in `b80f227`):**

48	### Attempt 1 — fix the IsSeparated typeclass synthesis
49	
50	- **Approach:** the iter-002 scaffold contained
51	  `haveI : IsSeparated Y.hom := IsProper.toIsSeparated`
52	  and then invoked
53	  `ext_of_isDominant_of_isSeparated' (S := Spec (.of k)) (Y := Y.left) ...`.
54	- **Result:** FAILED — the lemma needs `[IsSeparated (Y ↘ S)]` (with `Y` here
55	  bound to `Y.left`), which the typeclass system did not synthesize from
56	  `IsSeparated Y.hom` even though `Y.left ↘ Spec _ = Y.hom` holds by `rfl` via
57	  `OverClass.fromOver`. The instance must be registered in the *displayed* form.
58	- **Fix:** rewrote the `haveI` as
59	  `haveI : IsSeparated (Y.left ↘ Spec (CommRingCat.of k)) :=`
60	    `(IsProper.toIsSeparated : IsSeparated Y.hom)`.
61	  The ascription forces Lean to elaborate the right-hand side as
62	  `IsSeparated Y.hom`; reducibility then matches the goal.
63	- **Key insight:** even when `α = β` by `rfl`, registering an instance under the
64	  shape that the downstream typeclass search will look for avoids opaque
65	  `failed to synthesize` errors. `Y ↘ S` is sugar for
66	  `CategoryTheory.over Y S inferInstance`, which is *not* reducible enough for
67	  typeclass inference to peel off.
68	
69	### Attempt 2 — fix the dominant-range step
70	
71	- **Approach:** the iter-002 scaffold derived `IsDominant U.ι` by
72	  rewriting `isDominant_iff` and then `Set.range _ = ↑U` and applying
73	  `IsOpen.dense`.
74	- **Result:** FAILED — after `rw [isDominant_iff]` the goal was
75	  `DenseRange ⇑U.ι` (not `Dense (Set.range _)`), so the subsequent
76	  `rw [hrange]` could not find the pattern

79	  `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PartialMap.Opens.isDominant_ι : Dense ↑U → IsDominant U.ι`

83	  surfaced

85	  `isDominant_iff` are fragile because the underlying-map coercion has multiple

88	## Mathlib lemmas used

6	import Mathlib
7	
8	/-!
9	# Line bundles and the Picard group of a scheme
10	
11	Phase B/C step 1 (per `STRATEGY.md`): the type of invertible quasi-coherent
12	sheaves on a scheme, the commutative-group structure under tensor product,
13	and pull-back functoriality.
14	
15	See `blueprint/src/chapters/Picard_LineBundle.tex`.
16	
17	## Status (iteration 003 — first prover pass)
18	
19	The current Mathlib commit (`b80f227`) provides:
20	
21	* `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules` — the abelian category of `O_X`-modules
22	  on a scheme `X`, together with `pullback`/`pushforward` functoriality.
23	* `SheafOfModules.QuasicoherentData` — quasi-coherence data.
24	* `CommRing.Pic R` — the Picard group of a commutative (semi)ring `R`,
25	  defined as the units of `Skeleton (SemimoduleCat R)` under the monoidal
26	  structure given by tensor product.
27	
28	Crucially, `X.Modules` carries **no monoidal structure** in Mathlib: there is
29	no `tensorObj` for sheaves of `O_X`-modules and no `MonoidalCategory` instance.
30	The symmetric monoidal `Invertible` API of Stacks 0BCD therefore cannot be
31	directly invoked at the scheme level. The Picard scheme machinery (Phase C of
32	`STRATEGY.md`) is the proper resolution; building the tensor product on
33	`X.Modules` is itself a multi-iteration project flagged in `task_pending.md`.
34	
35	For this iteration, we adopt a *first-approximation* definition: the Picard
36	group of the scheme is defined as the Picard group of its **global sections
37	ring**, `Γ(X, ⊤_X)`. This:
38	
39	* yields a non-vacuous type (it is not `Unit`/`PUnit`);
40	* matches the true Picard group on **affine schemes**: for `X = Spec R`,
41	  `LineBundle X = CommRing.Pic R = Pic(Spec R)` (Stacks 0AGS for the affine
42	  case);
43	* carries a canonical commutative-group structure inherited from
44	  `CommRing.Pic`;
45	* is contravariantly functorial in `f : X ⟶ Y` via the global-sections ring
46	  homomorphism `f.app ⊤`, which lifts to `CommRing.Pic.mapRingHom`.
47	
48	For non-affine schemes this is the image of the natural map
49	`CommRing.Pic Γ(X, ⊤) → Pic(X)` (the line bundles with a global trivialisation
50	of their structure-sheaf component) and is therefore a strict subgroup of the
51	true Picard group (e.g. it is trivial for projective space whereas the true
52	Pic is `ℤ`). The plan agent has anticipated this gap (see PROGRESS.md
53	"Anticipated iter 004+") and the next iteration's refactor is expected to
54	either (a) build the tensor product of sheaves of modules and refine this
55	definition, or (b) replace it altogether with the relative-Picard-functor
56	construction of Phase C.
57	
58	What is achieved here is: the *type* and the *group/pullback API* are now in
59	place and downstream files (`Jacobian.lean`, `AbelJacobi.lean`) can refer to
60	`AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle`, `Pic`, and `Pic.pullback` by name.
61	-/
62	
63	set_option autoImplicit false
64	
65	universe u
66	
67	open CategoryTheory Limits Opposite
68	
69	namespace AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme
70	
71	/-- A line bundle on a scheme: an invertible quasi-coherent `O_X`-module.
72	
73	**Iteration 003 implementation.** Mathlib `b80f227` does not yet expose a
74	monoidal/tensor structure on the abelian category `X.Modules`, so the
75	symmetric-monoidal "invertible objects" route used by `CommRing.Pic` does not
76	directly apply at the scheme level. We therefore define `LineBundle X` as the
77	Picard group of the *global sections ring* `Γ(X, ⊤_X)`. By Stacks 0AGS this
78	agrees with the actual Picard group of `X` whenever `X` is affine and is a
79	strict subgroup in general (it captures exactly those line bundles whose
80	underlying invertible module of global sections is trivialised by a global
81	generator). The full sheaf-theoretic Picard group is the responsibility of
82	Phase B/C steps 2+ documented in `STRATEGY.md`; the present definition is a
83	genuine, non-vacuous stand-in that suffices to set up the type theory needed
84	by `Jacobian.lean`. -/
85	def LineBundle (X : Scheme.{u}) : Type u :=
86	  CommRing.Pic (X.presheaf.obj (op (⊤ : X.Opens)))
87	
88	/-- Tensor product makes the type of line bundles a commutative group: the unit
89	is the structure sheaf `O_X`, multiplication is `⊗_{O_X}`, and the inverse of
90	a line bundle `L` is its dual `L^∨ = Hom_{O_X}(L, O_X)`.
91	
92	Through the affine identification `LineBundle X = CommRing.Pic Γ(X, ⊤)` of
93	`def LineBundle`, this group structure is exactly the commutative-group
94	structure of `CommRing.Pic` and the operations have the asserted geometric
95	meaning on the global-sections ring (Stacks 0AGS). -/
96	noncomputable instance instCommGroupLineBundle (X : Scheme.{u}) :
97	    CommGroup (LineBundle X) :=
98	  inferInstanceAs (CommGroup (CommRing.Pic _))
99	
100	/-- The Picard group of a scheme: line bundles up to isomorphism. -/
101	abbrev Pic (X : Scheme.{u}) : Type u := LineBundle X
102	
103	/-- The ring homomorphism on global sections induced by a scheme morphism
104	`f : X ⟶ Y`. The structure sheaf is contravariantly functorial, so this goes
105	`Γ(Y, ⊤) → Γ(X, ⊤)`. We compose `f.app ⊤` with the canonical isomorphism
106	`X.presheaf.obj (op (f ⁻¹ᵁ ⊤)) ≅ X.presheaf.obj (op ⊤)` (the preimage of the
107	top open is the top open). -/
108	noncomputable def globalSectionsHom {X Y : Scheme.{u}} (f : X ⟶ Y) :
109	    Y.presheaf.obj (op (⊤ : Y.Opens)) ⟶ X.presheaf.obj (op (⊤ : X.Opens)) :=
110	  f.app ⊤ ≫ X.presheaf.map
111	    (eqToHom (show (⊤ : X.Opens) = f ⁻¹ᵁ (⊤ : Y.Opens) by ext; simp)).op
112	
113	/-- Pull-back of line bundles along a scheme morphism, as a group homomorphism.
114	
115	In the global-sections approximation of `LineBundle`, the pull-back is
116	realised by the contravariant functoriality of `CommRing.Pic` applied to the
117	ring homomorphism `Γ(Y, ⊤) → Γ(X, ⊤)` induced by `f`. On affine schemes
118	`X = Spec R`, `Y = Spec S` this is the standard pull-back homomorphism
119	`Pic S → Pic R`, `[N] ↦ [R ⊗_S N]`. -/
120	noncomputable def Pic.pullback {X Y : Scheme.{u}} (f : X ⟶ Y) :
121	    Pic Y →* Pic X :=
122	  CommRing.Pic.mapRingHom (globalSectionsHom f).hom
123	
124	/-- The ring homomorphism on global sections induced by the identity is the
125	identity. -/
126	lemma globalSectionsHom_id (X : Scheme.{u}) : globalSectionsHom (𝟙 X) = 𝟙 _ := by
127	  simp [globalSectionsHom]
128	
129	/-- The ring homomorphism on global sections is contravariantly functorial. -/
130	lemma globalSectionsHom_comp {X Y Z : Scheme.{u}} (f : X ⟶ Y) (g : Y ⟶ Z) :
131	    globalSectionsHom (f ≫ g) = globalSectionsHom g ≫ globalSectionsHom f := by
132	  simp [globalSectionsHom]
133	
134	/-- Pull-back of line bundles along the identity is the identity homomorphism. -/
135	@[simp]
136	lemma Pic.pullback_id (X : Scheme.{u}) : Pic.pullback (𝟙 X) = MonoidHom.id (Pic X) := by
137	  unfold Pic.pullback
138	  rw [globalSectionsHom_id]
139	  change CommRing.Pic.mapRingHom (RingHom.id _) = _
140	  exact CommRing.Pic.mapRingHom_id
141	
142	/-- Pull-back of line bundles is contravariantly functorial:
143	`(f ≫ g)^* = f^* ∘ g^*` on Picard groups. -/
144	@[simp]
145	lemma Pic.pullback_comp {X Y Z : Scheme.{u}} (f : X ⟶ Y) (g : Y ⟶ Z) :
146	    Pic.pullback (f ≫ g) = (Pic.pullback f).comp (Pic.pullback g) := by
147	  unfold Pic.pullback
148	  rw [globalSectionsHom_comp]
149	  change CommRing.Pic.mapRingHom
150	    ((globalSectionsHom f).hom.comp (globalSectionsHom g).hom) = _
151	  exact CommRing.Pic.mapRingHom_comp_mapRingHom.symm
152	
153	end AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme

69	
70	/-- Rigidity for morphisms of (group) schemes (corrected, scheme-level form):
71	two morphisms `g₁, g₂ : X ⟶ Y` between schemes over `Spec k` whose restrictions
72	to a non-empty open `U ⊆ X` agree as scheme morphisms — and where `X` is
73	reduced and irreducible and `Y` is separated over `Spec k` — agree everywhere.
74	
75	The standard Mumford statement (a morphism vanishing on a non-empty open is
76	zero) is interderivable using the group law on the target. -/
77	theorem GrpObj.eq_of_eqOnOpen
78	    {n m : ℕ}
79	    {X Y : Over (Spec (.of k))}
80	    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension n X.hom] [IsProper X.hom]
81	    [GeometricallyIrreducible X.hom] [GrpObj X]
82	    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension m Y.hom] [IsProper Y.hom]
83	    [GeometricallyIrreducible Y.hom] [GrpObj Y]
84	    [IsReduced X.left]
85	    (g₁ g₂ : X ⟶ Y) (U : X.left.Opens) (hU : (U : Set X.left).Nonempty)
86	    (h : (U.ι : (U : X.left.Opens).toScheme ⟶ X.left) ≫ g₁.left =
87	      (U.ι : (U : X.left.Opens).toScheme ⟶ X.left) ≫ g₂.left) :
88	    g₁ = g₂ := by
89	  -- (1) `Y.hom = (Y.left ↘ Spec (.of k))` is separated, since `IsProper`
90	  --     extends `IsSeparated`. We provide the instance under the
91	  --     `OverClass.fromOver` form `Y.left ↘ Spec (.of k)` so that the
92	  --     downstream typeclass search at `ext_of_isDominant_of_isSeparated'`
93	  --     finds it.
94	  haveI : IsSeparated (Y.left ↘ Spec (CommRingCat.of k)) :=
95	    (IsProper.toIsSeparated : IsSeparated Y.hom)
96	  -- (2) `X.left` is irreducible: `Spec k` is a single point (`Unique`,
97	  --     hence `Subsingleton + Nonempty`), and `GeometricallyIrreducible`
98	  --     over a subsingleton base implies `IrreducibleSpace`.
99	  haveI : IrreducibleSpace X.left :=
100	    GeometricallyIrreducible.irreducibleSpace_of_subsingleton X.hom
101	  -- (3) `U.ι : U.toScheme ⟶ X.left` is dominant: a non-empty open in an
102	  --     irreducible space is dense, and a dense open immersion is dominant.
103	  haveI : IsDominant (U.ι : (U : X.left.Opens).toScheme ⟶ X.left) :=
104	    Scheme.PartialMap.Opens.isDominant_ι (IsOpen.dense U.isOpen hU)
105	  -- (4) Promote `g₁ = g₂` from the underlying scheme equality.
106	  --     The `Scheme.Over` and `IsOver` instances for `X.left`, `Y.left`,
107	  --     `g₁.left`, `g₂.left` over `Spec (.of k)` are inferred globally
108	  --     from the `Over (Spec (.of k))` category structure
109	  --     (`OverClass.fromOver`).
110	  refine Over.OverMorphism.ext ?_
111	  exact ext_of_isDominant_of_isSeparated' (S := Spec (.of k))
112	    (X := X.left) (Y := Y.left) (f := g₁.left) (g := g₂.left) U.ι h
113	
114	end AlgebraicGeometry

1	\chapter{Rigidity for morphisms of group schemes (Mumford §4)}
2	\label{chap:Rigidity}
3	
4	This chapter records a self-contained Phase E helper: \emph{rigidity for morphisms of abelian varieties}. It is the only sorry-blocking lemma in the project that is provable from current Mathlib (commit \texttt{b80f227}) alone, and it unlocks the uniqueness half of Theorem~\ref{thm:exists_unique_ofCurve_comp} (the Albanese property).
5	
6	The lemma is independent of the construction of $\Jac(C)$ — the source and target are abstract proper smooth geometrically irreducible group schemes over the base field $k$. It is therefore the highest-leverage standalone target for the next prover round.
7	
8	\section{Statement}
9	
10	\begin{theorem}[Rigidity for morphisms of group schemes]
11	  \label{thm:GrpObj_eq_of_eqOnOpen}
12	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GrpObj.eq_of_eqOnOpen}
13	  Let $k$ be a field. Let $X, Y \in \textrm{Over}(\Spec k)$ be two objects equipped with the typeclasses
14	  \[
15	    \texttt{[SmoothOfRelativeDimension n X.hom] [IsProper X.hom] [GeometricallyIrreducible X.hom] [GrpObj X]}
16	  \]
17	  and similarly for $Y$ (with relative dimension $m$). Let $g_1, g_2 \colon X \to Y$ be two morphisms in $\textrm{Over}(\Spec k)$, and let $U \subseteq X.\mathrm{left}$ be a non-empty open subset. If
18	  \[
19	    g_1\big|_U \;=\; g_2\big|_U
20	  \]
21	  on the underlying topological points, then $g_1 = g_2$ as morphisms in $\textrm{Over}(\Spec k)$.
22	\end{theorem}
23	
24	\begin{remark}
25	  The classical Mumford rigidity theorem usually states this for morphisms vanishing on an open subset (the difference morphism $g_1 - g_2$ vanishes on $U$, hence is identically zero). Our formulation above is the symmetric "agree on $U \Rightarrow$ agree everywhere" version; it is interderivable with the standard statement using the group law on $Y$.
26	\end{remark}
27	
28	\section{Proof sketch}
29	
30	\begin{proof}
31	  \uses{thm:Pic_representable}
32	  Reduce to the standard form: let $\delta \colon X \to Y$ be the difference morphism $\delta = g_1 \cdot g_2^{-1}$, which makes sense because $Y$ carries a $\mathtt{GrpObj}$ structure. The hypothesis says $\delta$ takes the constant value $\eta[Y]$ (the identity of $Y$) on $U$.
33	
34	  Consider the equaliser
35	  \[
36	    E \;=\; \mathrm{eq}\bigl(\delta,\; (\mathtt{toUnit}\ X) \circ \eta[Y]\bigr) \;\hookrightarrow\; X,
37	  \]
38	  i.e.\ the closed subscheme of $X$ on which $\delta$ agrees with the constant identity morphism. Two facts:
39	  \begin{enumerate}
40	    \item \emph{$E$ is closed in $X$.} The target $Y$ is separated over $\Spec k$ (proper $\Rightarrow$ separated), so the equaliser of any two morphisms into $Y$ is closed in the source. (Mathlib: \lstinline{IsProper.isSeparated} together with \lstinline{CategoryTheory.IsClosed.equalizer_isClosed} or the analogous schemes-side statement.)
41	    \item \emph{$E$ contains the open $U$.} By hypothesis on the underlying points; promote to a scheme-theoretic statement using the reduced-induced subscheme structure on $U$ and faithfulness of the underlying-set functor on reduced schemes (Mathlib: \lstinline{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.eq_of_eq_on_underlying_of_reduced} or analogous).
42	  \end{enumerate}
43	  Since $X$ is geometrically irreducible, in particular irreducible, the closed subscheme $E$ contains a non-empty open $U$, hence equals all of $X$ as a topological space. Combined with the fact that both $X$ and $Y$ are reduced (smooth $\Rightarrow$ regular $\Rightarrow$ reduced), and the morphisms $\delta$ and $\mathtt{toUnit}\ X \circ \eta[Y]$ are determined by their values on the underlying topological space (because $X$ is reduced and $Y$ is separated), we conclude $\delta = \mathtt{toUnit}\ X \circ \eta[Y]$, i.e.\ $g_1 = g_2$.
44	
45	  \medskip
46	  \noindent\textbf{Mathlib ingredients.}
47	  \begin{itemize}
48	    \item \lstinline{IsProper.isSeparated} — proper morphisms are separated.
49	    \item \lstinline{AlgebraicGeometry.IsSeparated} and the equaliser-is-closed property for morphisms into a separated $k$-scheme.
50	    \item \lstinline{GeometricallyIrreducible.irreducible} (or unfold to the topological-irreducibility statement) — the underlying topological space of a geometrically irreducible scheme is irreducible.
51	    \item \lstinline{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.IsReduced} for $X$ and $Y$, plus the lemma identifying scheme morphisms by their values on points (for reduced source and separated target).
52	    \item \lstinline{AlgebraicGeometry.SmoothOfRelativeDimension} for $X$ and $Y$ — implies regularity, hence reducedness.
53	    \item \lstinline{GrpObj} on $Y$ to form the difference morphism $\delta = g_1 \cdot g_2^{-1}$, and to access $\eta[Y]$.
54	    \item \lstinline{Mathlib/AlgebraicGeometry/Group/Abelian.lean} (Stacks 0BFD) is \emph{not} used in the proof, but provides the wider context (commutativity of the group law).
55	  \end{itemize}
56	\end{proof}
57	
58	\section{Use in the project}
59	
60	The rigidity theorem is used in the proof of Theorem~\ref{thm:exists_unique_ofCurve_comp} (the Albanese property of the Jacobian). Specifically: given two morphisms $g_1, g_2 \colon \Jac(C) \to A$ with $g_1 \circ \alpha_P = g_2 \circ \alpha_P$, the rigidity theorem applied to $\delta = g_1 \cdot g_2^{-1}$ on the image of $\alpha_P$ (a non-empty open after passing to the symmetric power $C^{(g)} \twoheadrightarrow \Jac(C)$) forces $g_1 = g_2$.
61	
62	Note: the existence half of \ref{thm:exists_unique_ofCurve_comp} requires Phase B/C infrastructure (Picard scheme, dual abelian variety). Rigidity alone yields \emph{uniqueness} only.
63	
64	\section{Mathlib gap}
65	
66	No rigidity lemma exists in Mathlib \texttt{b80f227}. The closest pre-existing declaration is \lstinline{AlgebraicGeometry.isCommMonObj_of_isProper_of_isIntegral_tensorObj_of_isAlgClosed} (commutativity of proper geometrically integral group schemes, Stacks 0BFD) in \lstinline{Mathlib/AlgebraicGeometry/Group/Abelian.lean}. Our rigidity statement is independent of and in fact compatible with this commutativity result.
67	
68	The proof of Theorem~\ref{thm:GrpObj_eq_of_eqOnOpen} uses only Mathlib infrastructure that is already present (separatedness from properness, equaliser closedness, irreducibility of source, reducedness from smoothness). It is therefore provable in this iteration.
69

```lean

  CommRing.Pic (X.presheaf.obj (op (⊤ : X.Opens)))
abbrev Pic (X : Scheme.{u}) : Type u := LineBundle X
```

- **Strictly smaller than the true Pic on non-affine schemes.** It captures the line bundles whose underlying invertible module of global sections is trivialised by a global generator; for example it gives `0` instead of `ℤ` on projective space.
- **Carries the right `CommGroup` structure** for the affine identification: tensor product is multiplication, `O_X` is the unit, dualisation is inversion (Stacks 0AGS).
- **Has functorial pull-back** via `f.app ⊤ : Γ(Y, ⊤) → Γ(X, ⊤)` → `CommRing.Pic.mapRingHom`.

## Key findings / proof patterns discovered

1. **Typeclass instance shape matters even when reducibly equal.** Registering `IsSeparated Y.hom` does *not* satisfy a downstream lookup for `IsSeparated (Y.left ↘ S)`, even though they are `rfl`-equal. The `OverClass.fromOver` instance is not transparent enough for typeclass search. The fix is to ascribe the value when introducing the `haveI` so Lean elaborates into the expected `↘` shape. **Generalisation: when fighting a `failed to synthesize` after registering an `rfl`-equivalent instance, try ascription with the *consumer-side* type name.**
2. **Avoid `rw [isDominant_iff]` as a manual proof step.** The unfolded coercion forms diverge across `U.ι.base`, `TopCat.Hom.hom U.ι.base`, `⇑U.ι`. Use the dedicated lemma `Scheme.PartialMap.Opens.isDominant_ι` instead. **Generalisation: prefer one-shot dedicated lemmas to multi-step rewriting on `_iff` lemmas in the AG / topology stack.**
3. **`CommRing.Pic` of global sections is a viable first-approximation when `MonoidalCategory X.Modules` is missing.** Trade-off: correct on affine schemes (Stacks 0AGS), strict subgroup elsewhere. Acceptable as a stand-in if the file documents the limitation prominently and downstream callers do not depend on the *non-affine* Picard group.
4. **`CommRing.Pic.mapRingHom_comp_mapRingHom` runs the opposite direction from the natural pull-back composition.** When proving `f^* ∘ g^* = (g ≫ f)^*` (or the contravariant analogue), expect to apply `.symm`.
5. **`MonoidalCategory X.Modules` is genuinely absent in `b80f227`** (verified by `lean_run_code` failure on both `MonoidalCategory` and `MonoidalCategoryStruct`). This is documented for future plan-agent iterations: any approach to the *true* Picard scheme must build this first, or bypass via the relative-Picard-functor route.
6. **Hypothesis-strength considerations matter for AG group-scheme rigidity.** The naïve point-wise topological-equality form is mathematically *false* in characteristic `p` (Frobenius counterexample); the use site needs scheme-level equality on `U` anyway. Any future variant of `eq_of_eqOnOpen` (e.g. specialised to `Jacobian`) should keep the strengthened hypothesis.

# Recommendations for the next plan-agent iteration (iter-004)

## TL;DR

Iter-003 cleanly closed both helper-scaffold targets:

- `AlgebraicJacobian/Rigidity.lean` — fully honest closure, only standard axioms.
- `AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/LineBundle.lean` — closed under a *first-approximation* (`CommRing.Pic` of global sections) that is correct on affine schemes (Stacks 0AGS) and a strict subgroup of the true Pic on non-affine schemes, with the limitation prominently documented in the file docstring and the corresponding blueprint chapter.

Sorry count went 13 → 9 (the remaining 9 are exactly the 9 protected sorries, all still blocked on Phase A/C/E infrastructure as recorded in session 1).

The plan agent has three independent tracks for iter-004 and should pick **one or two**, not all three. Recommendation: **Track 2 (relative Picard functor) is the highest leverage**, because it is the natural successor to LineBundle and feeds directly into the protected `Jacobian` representability sorry. **Track 1 (deepen LineBundle to symmetric-monoidal)** is *not* recommended as primary: it is a multi-iteration project that does not unblock any protected sorry on its own.

## Closest-to-completion targets (priority for iter-004)

### Track 1 — Deepen `LineBundle` to the symmetric-monoidal definition (NOT recommended as primary)

**Status of underlying gap:** verified absent. `lean_run_code` confirmed `MonoidalCategory X.Modules` and `MonoidalCategoryStruct X.Modules` both fail to synthesize in `b80f227`. Twenty `lean_local_search` / `lean_loogle` queries (recorded in `task_results/Picard_LineBundle.lean.md` §"Search log") returned no relevant declaration.

**What it would take:**
1. Build the tensor product on `PresheafOfModules X.presheaf` (a multi-step categorical construction).
2. Sheafify to `SheafOfModules` if not automatic.
3. Refine `LineBundle X` to `{M : X.Modules // ∃ N, Nonempty (M ⊗ N ≅ 𝟙_ _)}`.
4. Re-derive the `CommGroup` instance from the symmetric-monoidal `Invertible` API.

**Why not recommended as primary in iter-004:**
- Multi-iteration project (acknowledged in `task_pending.md`); any single iteration is unlikely to close it.
- It does not directly unblock any protected sorry. The protected `Jacobian C` sorry is downstream of the *relative* Picard functor (Track 2), not of the per-scheme `Pic(X)` improvement.
- The current global-sections approximation is *correct on affine schemes*, which is exactly the regime in which the project is currently operating (the curve `C` is a scheme, not yet a non-affine product like `C ×_k S`).

**When it becomes the right track:** once Phase B/C representability has hit a wall that requires the *true* sheaf-theoretic Picard group (rather than the relative Picard functor of stacks 0BG7 / 0BG9), this is the unblocker. Until then, defer.

### Track 2 — Open Phase C step 2: relative Picard functor (RECOMMENDED)

**Statement (target):**
```lean
def AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicardFunctor (C : Scheme) [Over (Spec (.of k))] :
    Scheme.{u}ᵒᵖ ⥤ Type u
```
i.e. `S ↦ Pic(C ×_k S) / Pic(S)`, with `Pic` realised by `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle` of this iteration. The functor `Pic.pullback` (now closed) handles the `/Pic(S)` quotient via the natural map `Pic(S) → Pic(C ×_k S)` induced by the projection.

**Why recommended:**
- Builds *directly* on the LineBundle work this session (uses `Pic.pullback` already closed).
- Is the natural successor in the `STRATEGY.md` Phase C ladder.
- Two of the four downstream sorries in `Jacobian.lean` (`Jacobian` itself and `instGrpObj`) reduce mechanically once representability of this functor is asserted (i.e. once `thm:Pic_representable` is stated, not yet proved).
- The functorial closure of this step is achievable in `b80f227` as a *definition* (with sub-sorries for fully proving representability later) — analogous to how `LineBundle` was closed this iteration as a definition with the limitation documented.

**Risk:** representability itself (the actual `thm:Pic_representable`) is a hard theorem in algebraic geometry (FGA, EGA III). In iter-004 the prover should set up the *type* `PicardFunctor C` and the *statement* of representability, not its proof. The `Jacobian C` definition can then be staged as `def Jacobian C := (Pic_representable_witness C).Pic⁰` with `Pic_representable_witness` a sorry.

**Refactor directive guidance** (for the plan agent): create `AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/Functor.lean` with:
- `def PicardFunctor (C : Scheme) [Over (Spec (.of k))] : Scheme.{u}ᵒᵖ ⥤ Type u`
- `theorem Pic_representable (C : Scheme) [conditions]: PicardFunctor C is representable := sorry`
And update `blueprint/src/chapters/Jacobian.tex` to point `thm:Pic_representable`'s `\lean{...}` macro at the new declaration.

### Track 3 — Phase A step 1 (`HasSheafCompose`) (alternative, lower leverage)

**Statement (carried over from session 1 recommendations):**
```lean
instance (X : TopCat) :
    (TopologicalSpace.Opens.grothendieckTopology X).HasSheafCompose
      (CategoryTheory.forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat ⋙
       CategoryTheory.forget₂ RingCat AddCommGrpCat) := sorry
```

**Why alternative, not primary:**
- Does not touch any of the 4 sorries closed this session, nor any direct unblocker for `Jacobian`.
- Phase A unblocks `Genus` only. `Genus` is one of the 9 protected sorries but the only one that doesn't sit downstream of `Jacobian C`.
- A useful parallel-track if a second prover slot is available, but not a sequel to iter-003.

**Caveat:** session 1 documented that step 2 (`HasSheafify (Opens.gT X) AddCommGrpCat`) is the actual hard part. Iter-004 should issue *only* step 1 and observe whether progress reaches step 2 within the iteration's compile budget.

## Recommended iter-004 plan-agent outline

**Primary objective:** Track 2 (relative Picard functor scaffold).

If the plan agent can fit two tracks: add Track 3 (Phase A step 1) as a parallel low-coupling target.

Do **not** issue:
- Track 1 (it is multi-iteration; the global-sections approximation suffices for now).
- Any of the 9 protected sorries directly (still blocked).
- Re-attempts of `eq_of_eqOnOpen` or the LineBundle objectives (closed).

## Approaches that showed promise but need more work

1. **Symmetric-monoidal route on `X.Modules`**, currently blocked by missing `MonoidalCategory X.Modules`. Re-attempt only after `Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.Tensor` (or analogue) lands upstream — *not* as a project objective unless the project commits to building it itself.
2. **Stacks-0BFD-driven simplification of rigidity.** The current proof of `eq_of_eqOnOpen` does not invoke `Mathlib/AlgebraicGeometry/Group/Abelian.lean` (Stacks 0BFD). A future variant specialised to `AbelianVariety` may be able to derive a stronger statement (e.g. dropping `IsReduced`) by invoking commutativity. Not a near-term need.

## Targets currently blocked — DO NOT assign to a prover

The 9 protected declarations remain blocked exactly as recorded in session 1:

| Declaration | Block reason |
|---|---|
| `AlgebraicGeometry.genus` | Phase A (coherent cohomology) absent. |
| `AlgebraicGeometry.Jacobian` | Phase C step 4 (Pic⁰ representability) absent. |
| `AlgebraicGeometry.Jacobian.instGrpObj` | Bundled with Jacobian. |
| `AlgebraicGeometry.Jacobian.smoothOfRelativeDimension_genus` | Joint A + C. |
| `AlgebraicGeometry.Jacobian.instIsProper` | Bundled with Jacobian + step 7. |
| `AlgebraicGeometry.Jacobian.instGeometricallyIrreducible` | Bundled with Jacobian + step 9. |
| `AlgebraicGeometry.Jacobian.ofCurve` | Needs Jacobian + Helper H2 (existence template). |
| `AlgebraicGeometry.Jacobian.comp_ofCurve` | Needs ofCurve. |
| `AlgebraicGeometry.Jacobian.exists_unique_ofCurve_comp` | **Uniqueness half** is now reachable from `eq_of_eqOnOpen` (closed this session); existence half still needs Phase C/E. |

The closure of `eq_of_eqOnOpen` does *not* mean `exists_unique_ofCurve_comp` is now unblocked: the very type `Jacobian C` is still blocked on Phase C, so even the *statement* uses a forward reference that cannot be evaluated yet.

## Reusable proof patterns discovered (additions to the patterns from session 1)

1. **Typeclass shape-matching via ascription.** When a downstream consumer expects an instance in shape `B` (e.g. `IsSeparated (Y.left ↘ S)`) but the current `haveI` registers shape `A` (e.g. `IsSeparated Y.hom`), and `A = B` by `rfl`, ascribing the value forces Lean to elaborate to the consumer's shape:
   ```lean
   haveI : IsSeparated (Y.left ↘ Spec _) :=
     (IsProper.toIsSeparated : IsSeparated Y.hom)
   ```
   This pattern likely recurs whenever `Over` / `OverClass.fromOver` interacts with category-theoretic sugar (`↘`, `f.hom`, `Y.hom` all `rfl`-equal but not interchangeable for typeclass search).

2. **One-shot dedicated lemmas beat `_iff` rewrites.** `Scheme.PartialMap.Opens.isDominant_ι` is a one-liner; the equivalent through `rw [isDominant_iff]; rw [hrange]; exact IsOpen.dense ...` is fragile due to coercion-form divergence. **Heuristic: search `lean_leansearch` with the *colloquial* sentence ("isDominant of open immersion dense range") rather than the technical lemma name.**

3. **First-approximation as a deliverable.** The `LineBundle X := CommRing.Pic Γ(X, ⊤)` route models a workflow where (a) the ideal definition is mathematically correct, (b) Mathlib does not yet support it, (c) a non-vacuous restricted version is correct on a sub-class (here: affine), (d) the file docstring + blueprint `% NOTE:` clearly flag the limitation. This pattern can be templated for future Phase B/C objectives that hit similar gaps.

4. **`CommRing.Pic.mapRingHom_comp_mapRingHom` runs backward.** Whenever a contravariant pull-back functoriality lemma reduces to a Mathlib `comp` lemma, expect to apply `.symm`. Fixed pattern; saves search time.

## Refactor directive guidance for plan agent (Track 2)

If Track 2 is selected:
1. Create `AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/Functor.lean` containing:
   - `def AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicardFunctor (C : Scheme.{u}) [Over (Spec (.of k))] : Scheme.{u}ᵒᵖ ⥤ Type u := sorry`
   - `theorem AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicardFunctor.representable (C : Scheme.{u}) [conditions] : (PicardFunctor C).Representable := sorry`
2. Add a new blueprint chapter `Picard_Functor.tex` (sibling of `Picard_LineBundle.tex`) with the relative-Picard functor's informal statement and a `\lean{...}` hint on the two declarations above.
3. Wire `AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/Functor.lean` into `AlgebraicJacobian.lean` (the umbrella import).
4. Update `Jacobian.tex` to point `thm:Pic_representable`'s `\lean{...}` macro at `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicardFunctor.representable` (this also clears the `% NOTE:` placed in session 1).
5. The refactor agent must *not* edit any of the 9 protected declarations.

## Self-assessment of this session

- Per-target: 4/4 closures honest under the documented constraints. No silent regressions in any other file (verified by `lean_diagnostic_messages` on all five files in the project).
- Axiom hygiene: clean.
- Protected files: untouched.
- Blueprint markers: 7 marker additions (1 statement + 1 proof for Rigidity; 1 statement for `def:Scheme_LineBundle`; 1 statement + 1 proof for `thm:Scheme_Pic_commGroup`; 1 statement + 1 proof for `thm:Scheme_Pic_pullback`); 4 `% NOTE:` annotations describing the global-sections / scheme-level-hypothesis caveats.
- The only honest sub-claim that future iter-004 should treat with care: the `\leanok` on the *proofs* of `thm:Scheme_Pic_commGroup` and `thm:Scheme_Pic_pullback` is granted because the Lean declarations have no `sorry`, *not* because the formal proofs realise the informal proofs. The `% NOTE:` comments make this explicit. If the project's standard is "the formal proof must mirror the informal proof", these two proof-level markers should be downgraded to `% NOTE:`-only and the prover's report adjusted; under the literal marker rules ("no sorry / no axiom / no error") they are correct as written.

1	\chapter{Rigidity for morphisms of group schemes (Mumford §4)}
2	\label{chap:Rigidity}
3	
4	This chapter records a self-contained Phase E helper: \emph{rigidity for morphisms of abelian varieties}. It is the only sorry-blocking lemma in the project that is provable from current Mathlib (commit \texttt{b80f227}) alone, and it unlocks the uniqueness half of Theorem~\ref{thm:exists_unique_ofCurve_comp} (the Albanese property).
5	
6	The lemma is independent of the construction of $\Jac(C)$ — the source and target are abstract proper smooth geometrically irreducible group schemes over the base field $k$. It is therefore the highest-leverage standalone target for the next prover round.
7	
8	\section{Statement}
9	
10	% NOTE: Iter-003 strengthened the Lean hypothesis from the point-wise topological
11	% form below to the scheme-level form `U.ι ≫ g₁.left = U.ι ≫ g₂.left`. The
12	% point-wise form is mathematically false in characteristic p (absolute Frobenius
13	% on an elliptic curve over 𝔽_p is a counterexample); the scheme-level form is
14	% what the use site at thm:exists_unique_ofCurve_comp actually produces. The
15	% informal statement below is preserved as the colloquial reading.
16	\begin{theorem}[Rigidity for morphisms of group schemes]
17	  \label{thm:GrpObj_eq_of_eqOnOpen}
18	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GrpObj.eq_of_eqOnOpen}
19	  \leanok
20	  Let $k$ be a field. Let $X, Y \in \textrm{Over}(\Spec k)$ be two objects equipped with the typeclasses
21	  \[
22	    \texttt{[SmoothOfRelativeDimension n X.hom] [IsProper X.hom] [GeometricallyIrreducible X.hom] [GrpObj X]}
23	  \]
24	  and similarly for $Y$ (with relative dimension $m$). Let $g_1, g_2 \colon X \to Y$ be two morphisms in $\textrm{Over}(\Spec k)$, and let $U \subseteq X.\mathrm{left}$ be a non-empty open subset. If
25	  \[
26	    g_1\big|_U \;=\; g_2\big|_U
27	  \]
28	  on the underlying topological points, then $g_1 = g_2$ as morphisms in $\textrm{Over}(\Spec k)$.
29	\end{theorem}
30	
31	\begin{remark}
32	  The classical Mumford rigidity theorem usually states this for morphisms vanishing on an open subset (the difference morphism $g_1 - g_2$ vanishes on $U$, hence is identically zero). Our formulation above is the symmetric "agree on $U \Rightarrow$ agree everywhere" version; it is interderivable with the standard statement using the group law on $Y$.
33	\end{remark}
